turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you mean the opinion was written sua sponte or the holding was that it had no jurisdiction sua sponte? Courts write opinions sua sponte.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Okay. I'll accept the correction. The case was then sent back to the Federal Circuit. The Federal Circuit then wrote a lengthy opinion, most of which dealt with the subject of jurisdiction. It explained very jurisdiction. It specifically held, as it said in its first order, it had no jurisdiction pursuant to congressional statutory grant of jurisdiction, it was going to decide the merits, and in deciding the merits, it reversed the judgment below that was in favor of the Plaintiff and, of course, then after that, this Court granted certiorari. guestion of jurisdiction and the question was phrased whether the United States Court of Appeals may rule on the merits of an appeal when it expressly rules that it does not have subject matter jurisdiction pursuant to statute. That question was granted and the Court directed the parties to brief and argue a second question, which it did, in fact, the Federal Circuit have jurisdiction from the appeal. Now, with respect to the first question, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Lefstein, before you get to that, what coming to the conclusion that the Federal Circuit and not the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if we decide that the Court of Appeals shouldn't have decided the merits? Why shouldn't we just remand the case to them?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: To the Court of Appeals?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. MR- LEFSTEIN: Well, you might remand it to the Court of Appeals, but I assume that you would then have to give directions as to what would have to be done.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I guess the Court of Appeals would have some sense of its own about what ought to be done.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, your motives and ours may be somewhat different. I think probably this Court's interest is to see which of the two Courts of Appeals was correct' as to where this appeal should have gone to, and your motive is obviously to obtain a victory for your client, just like any good lawyer. But your appeal has -- it was not heard on the merits

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. heard on the merits --

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Well, wait a minute. I want to back

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay. merits. But the Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit did said we don't have jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it was your client -- well, no. It was your opponent who took the appeal -- was it you or your opponent that took the appeal originally from the Rock Island

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: The -- it was our opponent, Colt, the Defendant, who had a summary judgment entered against it by the District Judge, who took the appeal initially to the Federal Circuit, and then the Plaintiff, Charles Christianson, moved to transfer the case to the Seventh Circuit on the ground that the Federal Circuit did not have jurisdiction, and at that point, the Federal Circuit, after receiving briefs on that question, and presumably considering the whole issue, entered an order which specifically said that we don't -- I think the language was probably a three-line order, which said we see no basis for any jurisdiction in our court, and accordingly, and I'm paraphrasing now, we transfer the case to the United States decide, I gather, is which of the courts was the proper court for which the appeal should have been taken. questions. we send back the merits to whichever court we think should have had the appeal?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: That's obviously one option, but -- jurisdiction, the Federal Circuit has already decided the merits.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Well, if that's your answer, that's one of the approaches that you could, take. I might just mention that we have presented -- the first question actually states "may a court rule on the merits after it expressly States that it has no jurisdiction", and I suppose, technically, if you answer that question in the negative, and if you viewed that question as dispositive, then I think you could actually say that the Federal Circuit should not have decided it also. argue the second question, the question of jurisdiction.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor. That's correct. And if you view that one as dispositive -- the Federal Circuit as to jurisdiction, we could just say then that they shouldn't have decided the merits.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But then where does that leave the case?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Okay. If you agree --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There is no question presented as to the correctness of the decision on merits.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Well, there was a question --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There is none here. a question. In our prayer for relief at the conclusion of our briefs, we have suggested that if the Court should decide that the Federal Circuit was wrong in saying it did not have jurisdiction and, in fact, had jurisdiction, we have renewed our request that you accept certiorari on the merits. I realize that's strictly up to this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There has never been an appellate opinion

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Mo, that's incorrect. The Federal Circuit gave an opinion on the merits.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You do not question that if we decide that the Federal Circuit did not have jurisdiction, an available form of relief would be for us to simply send the case back to the Seventh Circuit? Could we do that? always assumed this Court in exercising --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could do any type -- oh, I see. policy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Well, the statutory power is under Section 1631, which indicates a transfer if it is in the interests of justice, and, so, we've made some arguments concerning the matter of whether it is in the interest of justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I renew my question. What do you want us

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Our preference is that you dismiss the directions that the appeal be dismissed. no jurisdiction?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: If you agree with us that it's not in the interest of justice to retransfer it, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the reason you say it's not in the interest of justice is because Colt made some misleading statements in characterizing your complaint?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But this was the same complaint that two circuits was inartfully drafted?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you also make a point they didn't cross petition. really seeking is a form of remedy which is a transfer back to the Seventh Circuit which could get Colt more or less relief than what they received from the Federal Circuit. They could qet more relief because they also had a claim for a cross motion for summary judgment. The Federal Circuit did not give them that relief, but, arguably, the Seventh Circuit could, so a transfer could get them more relief than, of course, under one of the cases we cite, I think the Federal Energy case. Even if there's going to be a modification which would give less relief, there's a complaint doctrine, the Seventh Circuit would still have jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You look at the original complaint and you don't even look at any substantive amendments?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Even if, arguably, you look at subsequent amendments, I think you still have to, under the well-pleaded complaint doctrine, and I think this is explained very careful in the Franchise Tax Board case, you're still dealing with what is the Plaintiff's cause of action. question of whether the trial court had federal jurisdiction. jurisdiction, and why does it make a lot more sense to say at the time of the appeal, let's see what was decided in the trial court and what is a fair constructive complaint at that time. is that when Congress set up the jurisdiction of the Federal Circuit, Congress didn't create some new standard for the Federal Circuit. What Congress said was the Federal Circuit's appellate jurisdiction is going to hinge precisely on the jurisdiction of the District Court. So, your question seemingly assumes that there's some different standard under Section 1295 which relates to the Federal Circuit, but 1295 jurisdiction hinges on whether there was a rise in under-jurisdiction in the District Court, and if

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that your answer is basically that even thought it might make a lot of sense, that's not what the statute does.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: That's a good answer. I think Congress has spoken on this, and if you read the reports which have been cited, and I might say, with all the disagreements that Colt and Christianson have in this case, right in their brief, they agree right at the outset that what we're dealing with are the traditional concepts of arising under jurisdiction and they cite the same congressional report that we cite, and that Congress specifically rejected some form of issue jurisdiction for the Federal Circuit as opposed to case jurisdiction because case jurisdiction is the standard under

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, sometimes it's hard to know the answer under the arising under standard. Should any special the Federal Circuit in deciding those guestions of its own jurisdiction or not?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: I think the concepts of arising under, although we have a hundred years of history with respect to those concepts, nonetheless, I think they are tests which probably any court is competent or should be competent to the Seventh Circuit did, simply is that we had an anti-trust complaint. We made no allegation, except there was one issue, this so-called 112 issue, and that paragraph didn't say, didn't make any claim at all that there were invalid patents.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the claim is that the case went forward in the District Court solely or principally on the patent issues.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Well, the case on --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And, so, it's as though the complaint

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Well, but, of course, using that analysis would contravene all of the years of teaching on this subject and as it culminated in Franchise Tax Board, which is that we look at the complaint, and the posture in which that

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What I'm saying is it's just as though there's an amended complaint filed because if the patent issues, we're predominated.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Well, if that was the case, then we clear holding in the language of Franchise Tax Board is that matter that's decided, if that didn't arise in the complaint, if that was a defense, then we don't have jurisdiction, and what we had here was an anti-trust

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Lefstein, suppose there are cases in which there is a tradition of construing federal jurisdiction narrowly, as the federal courts are courts of limited specialized tribunal who would decide patent issues because there was expertise there that wasn't share by the courts around the country. So, is it really compulsory that we follow the same strict analysis that would apply in the federal jurisdiction cases in a case like this? I think we're talking about appellate jurisdiction, too.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: I understand. I can only answer that by saying that Congress has spoken in this particular area.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You think they considered this problem of amended complaints and maybe a case does change its posture as it develops in the trial?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: I think they did, and what I base that on --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's the most illuminating discussion of

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: There is a statement and it's cited in but the statement in the congressional reports -- I think -- r'm not sure if it's the Senate or the House, it states that

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This is all very fanciful, isn't it?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: I can't speak to that. I assume Congress has some lawyers. Maybe they understand it, maybe they don't.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They wrote something that reads very much like 1331, though, doesn't it?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They wrote something that reads very much like the provision of the statute that uses the well-pleaded complaint doctrine. That's what they enacted, isn't it?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Yes, yes. Well, actually, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You don't know if they understood the well-pleaded complaint doctrine?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: I obviously can't speak to that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You think -- you want to make a guess about it?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: It depends what the experience of the lawyers were, who were on these committees. If they had occasion to go into the well-pleaded complaint doctrine. I think, though, on that statute, what I should emphasize is they didn't enact a new District Court jurisdictional statute. They left Section 1338 just as it was, and they said, we're going to peg the appellate jurisdiction to what the jurisdiction -- to what 1338 says.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would it make a whole lot of sense in if we want to ignore what Congress seemingly said, and even if situation where Congress also said we want to avoid undue specialization. There is discussion of that in the congressional reports. That we don't want one court that's just going to be deciding one particular thing, and I might also point out that in this particular area, what we're doing here with the 112 issue as it arose in this case, it is a federal pre-emption question, and the irony of the case is that the Federal Circuit has specifically said, and we cite the case, Cable Electric, and that we're going to follow the law of the regional circuit. So that had the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit actually come out with a different result on the merits, they would have looked to Seventh Circuit law to the extent that they could have divined it to determine whether or not there was federal pre-emption under the patent laws and, of course, this particular case is going to mean that if the result was different because of the exclusivity provision in Section 1338, which means that the states must hear -- where the states now have a right to hear cases involving patent as this, and the Federal Circuit gave an example, if there was a state law anti-trust case brought, that that would mean even though they've heard these cases for a hundred years because of that exclusivity provision, they would have to come out of the state courts and into the federal courts. So, I think an answer to the question that as a I don't think it's beneficial to bring all of the traditional cases that the states have held and bring them into the federal courts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask another question? The language Circuit if the claim was one that arose in whole or in part under 1338.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does that mean that if there's a single is, but under the patent, that that would be enough to -- that federal jurisdiction?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: No. I think that has to do with count. In other words, the in whole or in part language is not in Section 1338. The in whole or in part language is in Section 1295.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: So that you still have to have a genuine arising under case in your complaint, and I think we touch that in our reply brief, but, basically, we have a situation where we have solely an anti-trust case. So, there's no count at all that's based on the patent laws.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Counsel, what should the Federal Circuit have done here in your view when it gets this case back a second time?

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: We think they should have dismissed it and then Colt could have sought certiorari or they could have

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: No. I think that would have been unseemingly.

turns.name: STUART R. LEFSTEIN
turns.text: I think they could have certified it. I'd like to reserve my time.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Lefstein. Mr. Radice, we'll hear now from you.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The issue is whether the appeal of a judgment which invalidates nine patents, disgorges trade secrets, and imposes other liability as a remedy for patent law violations belongs in the Federal Circuit, Court of Appeals, or a regional circuit. In short, is this a case that arises under the patent laws ? The District Court's decision and judgment on liability held Colt liable for patent violations, specifically that it failed to comply with Section 112 of the Patent Act in failing to disclose all of the M-16's manufacturing specifications in connection with certain -- I'm sorry?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: It reached those issues because those are the issues presented to the District Court for summary

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: By?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: By the Plaintiff, by Christianson.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, didn't the Defendant rely on the patents ? argument was made that --

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: In the answer to the complaint, we did paragraph 18 of the complaint, which is the critical one as both the Seventh and Federal Circuits have recognized, is not allege patent validity and the patent invalidity. We responded with a denial and then a specific allegation of patent invalidity, but we understood the invalidity and trade secret invalidity based on 112, and we understood it that way because this was not the first time we there just a motion for summary judgment?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I believe our adversaries have announced

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, had there been a pre-trial conference outlining the issues?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: There had been conferences. Not a conference where the issues were outlined or where there was a pre-trial order, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: It's certainly a notation in the docket transcript made. Certainly not a pre-trial order outlining the issues. The District Court on the summary judgment motion held Colt liable for violating the patent laws and the relief or the remedy was the declaration of invalidity of nine patents and all of the trade secrets. There were no independent anti­ trust findings by the District Court or any trust questions analyzing it. The District Court decision thus contains the actual basis of liability imposed and defines the well-pleaded complaint. whether or not the allegations are well expressed in the complaint, we say, the District Court --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You say the District Court judgment

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: In setting forth the elements of liability that were imposed, Your Honor, particularly important here because the complaint was so incomplete and poorly drafted.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: 1338, I think as your opponent points out, it talks about having original jurisdiction of any civil action arising under any act of Congress relating to patents. Now, you say you can simply rely on the judgment?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: We are saying that -- two things, Your Honor. First, that you can use the decision, the judgment, the summary judgment motion to determine what are the necessary allegations of the complaint.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But ordinarily you think of 1338 as a grant of jurisdiction, and that people should know when the complaint is filed whether the Court has jurisdiction or not.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Ordinarily, and it would be the first issue if the question were state versus federal jurisdiction, but the alternative basis for jurisdiction here would be under So, there's no question of federal jurisdiction per se. So, the question didn't come up at that time. It was either, you know, patent --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but if you take your view, you would

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I use the judgment as just one way to interpret the complaint, Your Honor. The summary judgment motion itself, the theory of the summary judgment motion helps to interpret the complaint.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But lots of times, the theory of a case changes entirely from the time a complaint is filed, and this may have been one of them, till the time judgment is entered. So, you really got to address yourself as to what point in the

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: This is similar to Justice Stevens' question that an amendment, for example, of a complaint is something that comes after the complaint and would change, could change the jurisdiction of the District Court. A case that has no patent element in it could be amended -- amended complaint filed in the jurisdiction of the District Court at that point. After the beginning of the case, would be based, in part, on Section 1338, and the Federal Circuit has interpreted its jurisdiction in response to a question asked to my adversary, has interpreted its amendment jurisdiction this way. It has recognized amendments that either add a patent claim or delete a patent claim as effecting its jurisdiction and has very recently interpreted consolidation to have the the District Court? It wasn't just to invalidate the patents, was it?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: No. It went beyond that. It declared

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And wasn't that just a threshold step

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Perhaps a threshold step, but, nevertheless, the result was --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the relief — MR- RADICE: -- the validity which we could no longer enforce those patents.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the ultimate relief the Plaintiff was seeking and that the District Court was driving toward was damages under the anti-trust law and boycott theories, was it not? first with respect to the District Court? The District Court, in its decision, granted first the patent invalidity relief; second, the trade secret damage liability with a trial to be held at another time. So, it didn't reach the amount of damages. But all of that relief flowed, and this is inescapable from reading the District There were no independent anti-trust issues --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you saying that this case could not have been brought in state court under a state anti-trust law?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: It could not in the present form. Perhaps the best analogy would be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I don't know Illinois law, but suppose

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: This case as liability was imposed could not. This case would be a patent case because, fundamentally, liability was imposed solely as a basis --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No jurisdiction in the state court from the outset?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Because it is a patent case and, therefore, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's your position?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Yes, it is. got the complaint because, in fact, your answer to the complaint indicated that you didn't think there was a patent issue.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I think it's fair to say we didn't think about it at the time, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's what -- I mean, there you are. You say you admitted that Colt patents are valid until the end of each of their respective lifetimes. You regarded the complaint as not raising a patent issue.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Well, we also had -- if you're reading our response to paragraph 18, we also denied the allegation which we interpreted in paragraph 18 to be an assertion of patent and trade secret invalidity. So, we began by denying that allegation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Denying what? Denying what? The allegation generally, but when you come to talk specifically In other words, you're saying there's no patent issue in this case. That's what your answer says.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I think that the allegations may not match each other, Your Honor. Perhaps it's poor draftsmanship. It perhaps should have read as an averral of patent validity. Paragraph 18 is a confusing paragraph, and we would all have been better served had, as Judge Nichols said in his dissent, we called for an amended complaint at the time or a more definite statement of what the complaint was, but we did means, and it's our position that the way that one can -- the best way, the most reliable way to determine what the necessary allegations are of Plaintiff's real case is to look at the summary judgment motion, to look at the claim that was actually litigated and actually adjudicated. Whether we do that under the -- following the principles of the well-pleaded complaint rule, there are cases that go beyond the four corners of the bare words of the complaint to try to interpret what are the necessary allegations of the complaint, or whether we apply Rule 15(b) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, we come to the same conclusion. Rule 15(b), even without a formal amendment, requires

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's the end of the well-pleaded

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I don't think so, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We could call it the well-tried case doctrine because whatever comes out automatically is considered to amend the complaint and whatever the case shows is

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: If it comes up under 1295, only as regards the Federal Circuit's jurisdiction versus another appellate court's jurisdiction, and it's only at that stage that you have a record in between —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under 1331?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I think it's consistent, Your Honor. The cases -- I'm sorry. The Federated Department Store case, for been a prior action, the Plaintiffs had discontinued their action and tried to file the same action in state court, and the courts below and the Supreme Court affirmed, looked at the related litigation to determine what the real cause of action was, and, so, I think what we're saying is really consistent with the well-pleaded complaint rule, but it operates -- it certainly does not change the inquiry that must be addressed when the question is federal jurisdiction per se. That question must be addressed at the outset of the case where virtually all you have is the complaint. Perhaps a removal petition.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you have any -- I mean, looking to other litigation is really just trying to find out what the complaint historically meant. You can perhaps give it some clarity by looking at other litigation that was there at the same time, but that's quite different from saying we're going to look at the way the case developed. The way the case develops does not necessarily have the best indication if you're going to look outside its face of what the complaint meant is what kind of an answer did it provoke from the defendant. MR. .RADICE: But a case can change, particularly complex litigation, and, therefore, the jurisdictional basis can change.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. You are relying on the fact that a case can change while it's being tried.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: That's one thing, Your Honor. federal jurisdiction or not is fixed when the complaint is filed. So, you're not looking for historical fact what the complaint meant.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: It is fixed. Number one, it can change. Number two, we do read this complaint as a patent complaint and we did so at the time, and one of the reasons we did was because this was not the first time we had seen this issue. Christianson, into the Springfield litigation as a defendant, in The defense in that action was this Section 112 theory that our trade secrets and patents were invalid because of 112. The action went up on appeal, got a preliminary injunction, and we were upheld. The preliminary injunction was upheld. So that when we drop Christianson after we went back to the District Court, he turned around and filed this complaint, the instant complaint. We interpret it as turning the defense into an offense. We had seen the issue before. That's the way we understood it at the time. I certainly recognize this is not an easy complaint to read. It's not a model of draftsmanship, and that's why I suggest that looking at the subsequent proceedings assists the complaint really means.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, counsel, aren't there motions that you might have made during the course of the proceedings to force an amendment of the complaint or force a more definite statement? did not make them.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you didn't make them, and, yet, you want to rely on it as though those things had been done, and I'm not sure that's the waythe system is supposed to work.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Well, I think Rule 15(b) was written precisely for this situation, that where the parties had not recognized or the Court has not recognized the change in the theory between pleading and trial, that one -- de facto, it should be recognized and considered an amended pleading. We -- and the course of events was only about four or five months between this complaint and the bringing of the summary judgment motion, we cross moved for summary judgment motion, but it really just met the Section 112 theory. The motions didn't go beyond that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Wouldn't damages at the end of the line be calculated on an anti-trust theory no matter what happened to the patent?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Well, they could be. They could be.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Wouldn't they have to be?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Yes. We've never reached that stage. the diversity between the -- the divergence between the that it's solely a patent action, that it was brought as an action to declare patents invalid, which is a recognized action that arises under the patent laws, and that the relief went too far. The judge took the unprecedented step of patent violations and disgorged trade secrets. Secondly, to try to reconcile the original complaint, for example, with the decision, if it's an action that arises use would be the Walker Process kind of case. The Plaintiffs' complaint here arises -- it's -- I'm sorry. The action created by the anti-trust laws would be under Section 2 of the Sherman Act. The Plaintiff is proceeding to show that our patents are invalid and that the enforcement of those patents plus all the other elements of Section 2 would. come up. Certainly not at the Federal Circuit level.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And, in fact, the other circuits have retained jurisdiction in themselves, have they not? Ethicon.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: The closest case -- the only case that I'm aware of is the Handgards case, but -- I'm sure Your Honor is familiar with that one. I believe you were on one of the panels. At the time that issue was considered and the regional circuit kept the case, it appears to me from the decision that there was no longer a patent question in the ease . It's our position that where there is a patent case, the issue, the case arises under the patent laws and the appeal would still go to the Federal Circuit, and I believe with this . There is very little devoted in the Senate report to the hybrid-kind of patent anti-trust case, but there's a couple of things that are notable. There's a couple of statements in the Senate report, for example, that says specifically that they expect that all patent appeals to go to the Federal Circuit. They were more concerned -- there was some concern expressed that a frivolous patent appeal be attached to an anti-trust complaint and that the District Court should be able to deal with this, to prevent that steering jurisdiction to the Federal Circuit. But probably the most that is said on the subject is attached to the Senate Report. I think it's Appendix B, which is a letter from -- to Senator Dole from the Office of Court Administration that deals with the kind of mixed anti-trust and patent questions, and it characterizes most of these kinds of patent abuse claims, including the Walker Process claim, as fundamentally patent issues that it expected would go to the patent court, to the Federal Circuit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Radice, could I ask about -- I don't understand how you expect your Rule 15(b) argument to work when the proof shows that there is a patent claim the complaint is deemed amended to conform to that proof. half way -- I think I have jurisdiction because it doesn't seem to me that there's any patent limit and deep into the trial, it appears that we're getting into a patent issue and, boy, this case is really going to boil down, what do I do then. I transfer to the Federal Circuit.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: If you are on the Seventh Circuit, you don't get the case until the trial is over and a judgment has been written.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it doesn't arise at the District Court level? Why doesn't it arise at the District Court level insofar as the --

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: The District Court I do not believe either has the power nor is there a procedure for the District Court to determine the appellate court's jurisdiction. The next thing that would happen after the trial and the judgment is one of the parties has to file a notice of appeal, and the issue would come up if the other party questioned where that notice of appeal was directed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You say there's a constantly shifting jurisdictional basis in the District Court, but it doesn't make any difference.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Not constantly shifting. This is a pretty rare case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what if it started in a state court? You wouldn't have the luxury of letting the District Court

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Whether —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Doesn't the District Court have to know what law it's going to be governed by? Let's assume the Federal Circuit and the Seventh Circuit have quite different district judge says, oh, I'm back to the Federal Circuit now, and then the evidence changes, whoops, I'm back at the Seventh Circuit. This goes back and forth during the whole trial.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: On the choice of law question, at least the way the law seems to be developing, the Federal Circuit has taken the position that in areas outside its specialty, non­ patent areas, it would apply the law of the circuit. So, on the anti-trust issues, and this would be on an issue-by-issue basis, the theory would be that the -- whether in the regional circuit or the Federal Circuit, the federal -- I'm sorry. The regional circuit's anti-trust law should apply.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would apply anyway. So, it wouldn't matter.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Consistent with that principle is that, is that if a regional circuit got a patent issue is it should defer, there's a difference, because it has all the precedent. Circuit had jurisdiction and the rational --

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: That it had jurisdiction, but we say the proper rationale for its jurisdiction was best expressed in the Seventh Circuit's decision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Cross appeal?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: We did not because we did not submit a cross motion.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, my question is, you're not defending it?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: We are defending the result. We are not seeking any relief different from what -- you're defending.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: We are defending the judgment. We are not seeking relief or result different from what the Federal

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I think

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I think whichever way you rule, you necessarily rule on the other position because if the Federal Circuit had jurisdiction here, the Seventh --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Confusion. That's what you want. the Seventh Circuit are correct, but the judgment entered by the Federal Circuit was correct.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You have suggested the case, and I'm curious and I don't know if this is the law, but in your brief, you cite a case in which there was a patent claim asserted in the complaint, and then, during the course of the trial, the patent theory was abandoned, and the Federal Circuit held in the original complaint and they declined jurisdiction. Is that the general rule that's accepted, that they will -- when it will deprive them of jurisdiction to look at later amendments, they will not just look at the well-pleaded complaint, they will look at the latest.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I believe because there's been several cases on the subject in the Federal Circuit that's inconsistent. as I read the brief.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: They cited two or three, maybe both of those go the same way, but I think they have been consistent in recognizing that the amendment affects their jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What was the -- the Atari case, which was heard en banc, was one that also looked at subsequent developments, was it not? developments and in Atari, there was an attempt after the decision to separate the copyright case from which there had been a judgment and the patent part of the complaint and the Federal Circuit essentially held that that attempted separation was not affected. It's kind of a unique set of facts. Typically, a severance would occur much earlier. It really didn't reach the question of if there was an effective severance that resulted in separate trials and separate judgments, how that would affect its jurisdiction. something your brief, that says they looked at the situation at the time of the appeal rather than the time of the complaint, which is also consistent with your view that you start out with a complaint alleging a patent violation and then later amend it I think that's -- I don't think that does violence to the well- pleaded complaint rule, Justice Stevens. I think that that's what courts have done where they are confronted with either a poorly-drafted complaint or a changed theory, and I think the opposite result being wedded to the complaint would do violence to the congressional purpose here because you could manipulate complaint, abandon it, and throughout the history of the case, all appeals would go to the Federal Circuit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, even if you say that you can use it as a practical matter in this area, you certainly couldn't apply the well-pleaded complaint doctrine generally as far as federal versus state jurisdiction goes in that fashion, could you, because then a federal court would not know during the course of a trial whether it had jurisdiction or not? So, you're arguing for a different well-pleaded complaint rule here --

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I don't think there's been any inconsistency. If a patent claim was attached, let's say, to a state claim and the court assumed jurisdiction and then determined that the patent claim was frivolous, I don't believe the District Court is supposed to proceed with jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, let's leave patent claims out of it. The issue is just whether this is a federal case or not before federal issue and during the course of the trial, sometimes it seems there is and sometimes it seems there isn't. You're saying that you can look to the end of the trial to determine whether the federal court has jurisdiction.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I'm saying that that is the most advisable rule to determine appellate jurisdiction. jurisdiction as well, doesn't it?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: It does. It does.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you're asserting that the principle you're now espousing is what is used in determining whether a federal court has jurisdiction, that you look at how the trial goes, and if the decision is ultimately based on a federal question, it had jurisdiction, and if it isn't, it didn't. Is changes minute-by-minute. This is a rather unique case where

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm talking generally and never mind the changing. At the end of the trial, it appears that there was a federal claim because of new evidence brought in, although the complaint clearly on the face of the complaint there wasn't under standard well-pleaded complaint doctrine.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: The Court tried a federal question case, in this case, a patent question case, yes, we should look to that to determine appellate jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you have any cases, aside from this patent area?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Not specifically.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Any case at all that does --

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: By operation of Rule 15(b).

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You have cases that you think the case would say that?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: The language of Rule 15(b) amends the complaint, but this situation I don't think could come up under normal federal jurisdiction per se because jurisdiction is determined at the outset. It could only come up when one has the advantage of a retrospective analysis. determined the jurisdiction of the temporary emergency Court of Appeals and Judge Newman, which ultimately determined that it was issued jurisdiction, said that the appellate court should take the advantage of looking in retrospect and not deal just with the frozen words of the original complaint because it is better able to determine whether that is the kind of case that should go to one Circuit Court of Appeals or the other. has jurisdiction. What about the decision on the merits? Do you concede have reason to seek cert here for review of the merits?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: I do not think so. It's a decision that I think is, of course, on the merits, eminently correct. It applies traditional patent law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but we have not had any appellate review of that decision, have we?

turns.name: ANTHONY M. RADICE
turns.text: Has had -- we both have had appellate merits and it's been fully briefed and argued. Obviously, the Court can accept certiorari on the merits. We think the related point is that, you know, the question as to my adversaries, what does the Court do when it finds that the Seventh Circuit really had jurisdiction, and there's no support for a dismissal. In fact, the Harley-Davidson v. Buffington case that we cited in our briefs says that our right to an appellate -- right to an appeal means a right to have an appellate determination. There is precedent for what this Court could do in at determined that the D.C- Circuit did not have jurisdiction, but the Federal Circuit did. It simply remanded and directed a REPORTERS' CERTIFICATE DOCKET NUMBER: 87-499 CASE TITLE: Charles R. Christianson v. Colt Industries HEARING DATE: April 18, 1988 LOCATION: Washington, D.C. HERITAGE REPORTING CORPORATION Washington, D. C. 20005
